data.title: ANGL: Higher Quality Outperforms Amid Volatility And Spike In Downgrades
data.description:
Fallen angels ended Q1 strong, outpacing rising stars with a 21% market value gain and driving a notable shift in market composition.

Fallen angels (as represented by the ICE US Fallen Angel High Yield 10% Constrained Index, "H0CF") finished Q1 on a strong note, outperforming the broad high-yield market (as represented by the ICE BofA US High Yield Index, "H0A0") by 0.60% (1.55% vs. 0.95%). Increasing concern around U.S. trade policy and the expected negative impacts on economic growth and inflation drove high yield credit spreads to their highest levels since August 2024. Spreads have continued to widen in the first few days of April, very close to the October 2023 levels (high yield spreads ~450bps). Higher quality credit outperformed in this more uncertain environment, benefitting fallen angels relative to the broad market, given their approximately 26% overweight to BB rated bonds and 6% underweight to CCC and below rated bonds. CCCs are now down -0.67% YTD after also underperforming in February, while higher quality, BB-rated bonds outperformed each month in the quarter at 1.29%, 0.72% and -0.56% respectively. Despite strong balance sheets and fundamentals overall, spread levels remain historically tight even after this recent widening, making high yield (especially lower rated bonds) vulnerable to continued price declines as the market digests the impact of tariffs and amid generally increased uncertainty. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield fluctuated during the period and ended the quarter at 4.21%, a decline of 0.32%. The yield curve steepened in March, with a higher degree of rate cuts being reflected in the 2-year rate, but 2yr/10yr ended the quarter approximately where it began, and overall longer duration exposure outperformed.

BB- Rated Bonds Continue to Outperform

Fallen Angels Overall Statistics: Fallen angels and broad high yield spreads widened in the first quarter of the year by 8bps and 63bps, respectively, as lower rated bonds exhibited wider spreads, particularly in March, with CCC & below rated bonds widening by 168bps. The widening reflects an escalating trade war and policy uncertainty, along with increased odds of a U.S. economic slowdown and higher inflation. Yields remained relatively high for both indices, although fallen angels finished the quarter slightly lower than the beginning of the year. Broad high yield, on the other hand, ended the quarter with wider spreads offsetting a decline in risk-free yields, due to a 45% exposure to single-B or below-rated bonds. Fallen angel stats were impacted by the addition of new fallen angels equating to approximately 20% of added index weight. The new entrants (Nissan Motor Acceptance, Nissan Motor and Celanese US Holding) entered with lower yields and spreads relative to the overall index.

Fallen Angels: Aptiv PLC (APTV) junior subordinated debt was downgraded by Fitch to BB+ from BBB- in January, as the company is planning to spin off its electrical distribution systems business into a separate company. Fitch stated that the downgrade aligns more closely with other similarly junior subordinated debt from other issuers. Over the last six months, the junior subordinated debt price decreased to $99.71 from $101.05. Within the Auto sector, S&P affirmed Ford's BBB- rating in early February but revised their outlook to negative as its expansion over the next couple of years appears to be limited due to slower-than-expected progress on cost reduction, high labor costs and increasing pricing pressure. S&P stated that the negative outlook also reflects an increase in the risk of downgrade over the next 12-24 months, which could make Ford a fallen angel again, all else equal, as Moody's credit rating is Ba1 while Fitch is BBB-. In February, Celanese U.S. Holdings was downgraded by Moody's to Ba1 from Baa3, with a negative outlook, following the downgrade by S&P in November to BB+. The downgrade by Moody's was due to concerns over the company's increased debt levels and potential challenges in maintaining strong cash flow amid market uncertainties and reflects apprehensions about Celanese's ability to reduce leverage while navigating fluctuating demand and pricing pressures in the chemical industry. Moody's and Fitch also downgraded Nissan (OTCPK:NSANF) to high yield with a negative outlook in February, due to declining profitability linked to weakening demand for its older vehicle lineup, especially in markets such as the U.S. and China. Moody's also raised concerns about Nissan's ability to effectively execute its restructuring strategy, including workforce reductions and production cuts, amid ongoing global economic uncertainty while Fitch stated that persistently low profitability and a recovery trajectory that has been slower than anticipated.

Despite the concentration in Autos over the past few months, fallen angels have generally been idiosyncratic in nature. We are keeping an eye on signs for increased systemic downgrade activity, given the increased macro uncertainty following the recent tariff announcement.

Rising Stars: Western Alliance Bancorp (WAL), a fallen angel from the regional banking crisis in early 2023, exited the index in February as Moody's upgraded it back to investment grade, recognizing the company's stronger capital position and improved risk management practices. The upgrade reflects increased resilience to potential financial stress and a more robust ability to navigate economic uncertainties. Back in April 2023, Western Alliance joined the fallen angel index with a price of $76.39 and now leaves at $93.75, providing an approximate 23% return over its time in the fallen angel index.

data.title: KBWY: The High Yield Isn't Sustainable
data.description:
Overview

REITs are still one of my favorite places to obtain a high dividend yield. I want to grow my passive income with the most hands off approach possible, so physical rental properties have slowly become less desirable to me. I hold many high quality REITs and have been searching for the most effective way to consolidate some of these positions. Invesco KBW Premium Yield Equity REIT ETF (NASDAQ:KBWY) operates as an ETF that focuses on a diversified group of REITs while offering a double-digit dividend yield. The high dividend yield of about 10.8% initially caught my attention, but after looking at prior reporting, I am cautious about the sustainability of the dividend.

Looking at the performance over the last five years, we can see that KBWY's price has fallen by about 13.5%. When including all of the distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 29% over the same period. A nice aspect of KBWY is that the distributions are paid out on a monthly basis, which can be great for the income-seeking investors. However, the dividend payout amounts have trended downward over the last ten years and I do not see a catalyst for growth in the near term.

Despite the recent price decline, I do not believe that KBWY offers an attractive value proposition to investors at this time. The underlying holdings could use some improvement, as it looks like the fund doesn't actually generate enough earnings to support the distribution. Additionally, KBWY remains heavily suppressed during in this higher interest rate environment. The fund has an inception dating back to 2010 and has an expense ratio of 0.35%. So let's start by taking a look at KBWY's underlying strategies and holdings that it utilizes to generate earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, KBWY has assets under management of only $188M that is spread across 30 different holdings. We can see that the largest focus is on diversified REITs making up 25.14% of assets. This is followed by a focus on Health Care REITs and Office REITs, accounting for 17.39% and 17.26% of assets respectively. The combination of these three sectors makes up nearly 60% of the entire fund, so we can assume these will have the greatest impact on KBWY's daily price change and total return potential.

Additionally, I am not a fan of the focus on Office REITs, as the demand for these sorts of properties have significantly decreased over the last five year span. A big contributor to this is the growing acceptance to remote working opportunities. There is plenty of data to support that the demand will likely decrease over the next decade.

From a top holdings perspective, Global Net Lease (GNL) is the largest position at 6.98% of assets. This is followed by a 6.15% weight in SITE Centers (SITC) and a 5.41% weight in Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN). Since the top ten holdings make up over 45% of the fund, I would suggest that investors put some time into researching these top holdings since they are likely to have a big influence on the fund's success. Just as a starting point here are some notable callouts for some of the holdings:

  • Brandywine Realty Trust: share price has fallen by more than 75% over the last decade. Currently, has an F-rating in terms of profitability.
  • EPR Properties (EPR): excellent dividend coverage and a growing portfolio of properties. I did previously cover ERP and issued a Buy rating.
  • CTO Realty Growth (CTO): currently has a strong buy rating from Wall Street analysts. There is a potential upside of 31% based on the average price target.
  • Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH): has declined by more than 45% since inception and recently reduced its dividend by 32%.

Ultimately, I think the holdings offer a mixed bag. There are some high quality REITs in combination with some REITs that I consider to be more speculative in nature. Perhaps this approach may be favorable to you, but for me I prefer to have a portfolio of holdings that have proven and consistent track records of growth.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since KBWY's launch, there hasn't really been an extended period of time where the share price has risen. The only exception would be between 2020 and 2021, when interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic. An extremely low interest rate meant that real estate companies were able to acquire very cost efficient debt financing, which could be allocated towards different growth initiatives. Lower rates also created an environment where the cost of any floating rate debt held on the balance sheet became cheaper, essentially improving operating margins for many of these businesses. This is why KBWY experienced growth throughout 2021 when rates were low.

The Fed started to aggressively hike interest rates throughout 2022 and 2023 in response to record-breaking inflationary data. As a result, KBWY's price started to decline since the cost of debt was higher, which shrank operating margins and made it more difficult to grow. We still remain in one of the highest interest rate environments over the last decade, which, I believe, will continue to be a vulnerability for KBWY.

Inflation still remains higher than anticipated, so the Fed has adjusted their outlook on rate cuts. There is now only estimated to be one interest rate cut to take place over 2025. Depending on which direction inflation goes, there's even a chance that we see interest rates get increased at some point over 2025. For this reason, I do not believe that KBWY offers an attractive entry point yet.

Similarly, the most updated reporting available in the semi-annual report dated from February 2024. With the exception of 2021, KBWY has experienced net realized losses every single year since inception. Net investment income levels have also declined since its peak in 2020. Therefore, KBWY's NAV has continued to fall over time and now sits at its all-time low. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2019: $29.25
  • 2020: $18.71
  • 2021: $23.91
  • 2022: $22.07
  • 2023: $19.15
  • 2024 (semi-annual): $17.34

A declining NAV is one of the clearest indications that the underlying strategy isn't working for KBWY. The NAV decline is also exacerbated by the fact that the fund puts an emphasis on paying out distributions to shareholders. Since the fund isn't actually able to generate enough earnings to support the distribution, the NAV declining at a much faster rate.

Questionable Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.1262 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.8%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by 12, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.5144 per share. Referring back to the same report mentioned above, the fund has only been able to generate earnings in excess of this amount once. For reference, here are the annual earnings total:

  • 2019: ($4.65) per share
  • 2020: ($8.26) per share
  • 2021: $6.63 per share
  • 2022: ($0.49) per share
  • 2023: ($1.34) per share
  • 2024 (semi-annual): ($1.03) per share

As a result, the dividend history reveals that the payout amounts have been on a slow decline over the last decade. So despite a generous starting dividend yield, KBWY isn't actually a great choice for investors seeking a source of reliable income. I anticipate the payouts to continue trending downward over time until the interest rate environment becomes more favorable.

Since distributions are funded by net investment income, the dividends received are classified as ordinary dividend income. Ordinary dividends have the least favorable tax consequences when compared to the qualified dividends that you'd receive from more traditional dividend paying companies. Therefore, a position in KBWY would be best utilized within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am rating KBWY as a sell due to the continuous decline of the NAV and unsustainable dividend coverage as a result of the poor mix of holdings. While there are some solid REITs within KBWY, the ETF includes some less desirable holdings that are more speculative in nature. KBWY has demonstrated a clear vulnerability to the current high-interest rate environment, and the appeal is limited since the market doesn't anticipate interest rate cuts to happen anytime soon.

data.title: QGRO: A Quality Growth ETF That's Winning
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered the American Century U.S. Quality Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:QGRO) on December 12, 2023, rating it a "buy" after noting the positive impact its May 2023 Index change had on the portfolio's fundamentals. Since that review, QGRO has outperformed most large-cap growth funds, without much help from top mega-gap stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL).

I've read other articles covering QGRO since and was surprised to see other analysts not acknowledge the Index change, instead choosing to rely on its performance since its inception to help justify their conclusions. This article is different. Rather than letting historical price charts cloud my outlook, I'll look at what makes QGRO work from a factor perspective. With this information, I hope I can convince you that QGRO is actually one of the strongest all-around large-cap growth funds on the market, and if you're intent on limiting exposure to the stock market's largest companies, it might be the best one available, period. I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

QGRO Overview

Strategy Overview and Composition

Effective May 31, 2023, QGRO tracks the American Century U.S. Quality Growth Index, which, according to its fact sheet, enhances core growth exposure by:

  • Identifying top growth companies that feature sound fundamentals.
  • Allocating dynamically to stable and high-growth stocks depending on the market environment.
  • Seeking to manage risk through position limits and by emphasizing larger, less volatile companies.

The last point is understated. According to a composition analysis comparing QGRO with the four ETFs listed earlier (IWF, SCHG, VUG, SPYG), QGRO has substantially less exposure to the top 10 holdings common to all funds. As of April 9, 2025, total exposure to these ten stocks is only 16% compared to 57% on average.

Index Methodology Summary

Based on the Index methodology document, updated as of November 2024, I've summarized the following key points of the selection process below.

1. American Century Investment Management is the Index provider and is responsible for constructing two portfolios from the securities in the S-Network US Equity Large/Mid-Cap 1000 Index, excluding the bottom 2% by market cap: 1) Stable Growth and 2) Pure Growth, with the final portfolio being a blend of these two sub-portfolios.

2. The Index Provider ranks each security by quality and excludes stocks ranked in the bottom 25% in aggregate and in the bottom 25% within their respective sectors. The attributes used are summarized below and are combined to create a quality score for each security.

3. The Index Provider assigns each security a value score based on the attributes below.

3. The Index Provider assigns each security a growth score based on the attributes below and removes the bottom 40% of companies in aggregate.

4. The Index Provider creates a Pure Growth sub-portfolio that maximizes the growth score, subject to the following constraints:

  • Sector: 50-200% of the market sector weight
  • Quality: At least 15% above the market
  • Growth: At least 30% above the market
  • Market Cap: At least 42% of the cumulative market cap

5. The Index Provider creates a Stable Growth sub-portfolio that maximizes the value and profitability composite scores, subject to the following constraints:

  • Sector: 30-150% of the market sector weight
  • Quality: At least 15% above the market
  • Growth: At least 15% above the market
  • Market Cap: At least 42% of the cumulative market cap

6. Each sub-portfolio is subject to security constraints, as follows:

  • Individual Stock Weights: At least 0.25%
  • Size and Volatility: grouped with an upper weight between 0.5-3.5%
  • Top Size Decile Weights: Maximum 15% less than the stock universe

7. The Final Index is a blend of the two sub-portfolios, with each sub-portfolio weighted between 35-65%, with no month-to-month change over 15% permitted. Portfolio weights are based on trailing returns adjusted by volatility when returns are positive and trailing returns only when returns are negative.

Key Takeaways

It's a lot to absorb, but my key takeaways are that quality and growth exposure must be at least 15% above the market for both sub-portfolios. In addition, the individual security and sector constraints promote diversification, and taking into consideration a company's value features ensures limited exposure to the mega-cap growth stocks that dominate the major indices. As I'll touch on shortly, total overlap with the other ETFs is only 32-33%.

Performance Snapshot

Earlier, I provided QGRO's performance track record since my last review date against the following four peers:

  1. iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF)
  2. Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)
  3. Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG)
  4. SPDR S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG)

With only $1.16 billion in assets under management, QGRO is tiny compared to these giants, but that doesn't mean it's inferior. Since its Index change on May 31, 2023, it's been the top performer in this peer group, gaining 39.16% compared to 34.73% for IWF, which is probably its closest peer. SCHG was second-best at 37.29%, followed by SPYG and VUG at 35.87% and 34.80%.

One drawback to QGRO is higher historical volatility, evidenced by its 18.30% annualized standard deviation figure from June 2023 through April 10, 2025. However, its downside risk-adjusted returns (Sortino Ratio) were still the best.

QGRO Analysis

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between QGRO, IWF, SCHG, VUG, and SPYG. Right away, you'll notice QGRO isn't nearly as tech-heavy as the other funds. Its 31.57% total exposure is 12.65% below the peer group average, with the offset roughly evenly distributed between Health Care (+5.23%) and Industrials (+5.82%).

Boston Scientific (BSX), QGRO's #11 holding, is the largest overweighted position in the Health Care sector, with a 2.34% allocation that's 2.04% above average. The Industrials sector is led by Uber Technologies (UBER) at #9, but what's most important is the minimal Magnificent Seven representation. Meta Platforms (META) and Netflix (NFLX) are there, but others, like Amazon (AMZN), Nvidia (NVDA), and Apple (AAPL), are #12, #15, and #23 on the list. QGRO's total Magnificent Seven exposure is only 11.60% compared to 49.00% on average for the other four funds.

QGRO Fundamentals: Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for QGRO's top 25 holdings, which comprise 56.55% of the portfolio. Again, this demonstrates its excellent diversification features, as this concentration level is about 16% below the peer group average.

Here are three additional observations to consider:

QGRO: Strong Bottom-Line Growth Rates

QGRO's components have a weighted average 23.87% one-year estimated earnings per share growth rate, which is about 1-2% better than the peer group. As QGRO's top stock, Booking Holdings (BKNG) is one example of an opportunity that's often ignored by growth ETF investors due to its relatively low market cap. Analysts expect 26.35% earnings growth over the next year, and due to its global operations, I believe it's well-positioned to defend against President Trump's tariff policies. Gross travel bookings grew 10% last year to $166 billion, and the company has resumed its steady growth rate that was halted in 2020 due to the global pandemic.

One drawback is that QGRO's three-year historical and one-year forward sales growth rates are a few percentage points below average, implying that its holdings must either cut costs or buy back shares at a faster rate. Still, using Seeking Alpha Growth Grades as a guide, I calculated a 6.21/10 sector-adjusted growth score for QGRO, which ranks #20/77 in the large-cap growth category, per my April 2025 ETF Rankings. It's a great score for such a diversified fund, and I attribute this to how American Century calculates growth using both historical and expected cash flow growth. On that note, I also calculated three-year historical free cash flow growth rates for each fund, which puts QGRO way ahead of the competition, as follows:

  • QGRO: 18.44%
  • IWF: 13.30%
  • SCHG: 12.99%
  • VUG: 12.84%
  • SPYG: 14.34%

QGRO: Quality Is Solid But Not As Good As Peers

Many analysts overlook the cost of diversification with regard to quality. For example, while the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) is obviously far more diversified than the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), its net margins are also about 7% lower (14.10% vs. 21.47%), so that's going to offset some of the safety benefits an equal-weight strategy offers. We must remember that 24/25 of SPY's top holdings have perfect "A+" Seeking Alpha Profitability Grades, so if we're going to deviate from that, we should demand a superior factor mix.

Based on my calculations, there's no doubt that QGRO is a lower-quality fund compared to IWF, SCHG, VUG, and SPYG. I listed a few quality statistics above, like QGRO's components' 16.91% return on total capital and 19.70% free cash flow margins, but to complete the picture, please consider these additional metrics that indicate QGRO is below average almost across the board. The one bright spot is a slightly lower debt-equity ratio, but debt as a percentage of free cash flow is relatively high, and its components' interest coverage ratio is 8.63 points below average, so I don't think it has any real quality advantage worth touting.

We know QGRO's quality is below average, but the question is if it's fair for its size. To find out, I calculated sector-adjusted profit scores for other large-cap growth ETFs with market caps between $100 and $500 billion and found the following 13:

  1. Pacer Nasdaq 100 Top 50 Cash Cows Growth Leaders ETF (QQQG): 9.52/10
  2. Pacer US Large Cap Cash Cows Growth Leaders ETF (COWG): 9.32/10
  3. VictoryShares Free Cash Flow Growth ETF (GFLW): 9.28/10
  4. American Century U.S. Quality Growth ETF (QGRO): 9.21/10
  5. VanEck Morningstar Wide Moat Growth ETF (MGRO): 9.19/10
  6. First Trust Growth Strength ETF (FTGS): 9.10/10
  7. Direxion NASDAQ-100 Equal Weighted ETF (QQQE): 9.00/10
  8. First Trust NASDAQ-100 Equal Weight ETF (QQEW): 9.00/10
  9. Invesco S&P 500 Pure Growth ETF (RPG): 8.99/10
  10. Invesco S&P 500 GARP ETF (SPGP): 8.90/10
  11. FT Raymond James Multicap Growth Equity ETF (RJMG): 8.62/10
  12. Sterling Capital Focus Equity ETF (LCG): 8.44/10
  13. First Trust Large Cap Growth AlphaDEX ETF (FTC): 8.33/10

QGRO's 9.21/10 profit score ranks #4/13, and it's worth noting that it also ranks #2/13 on diversification. As I've stated before, it's easy to design a high-quality fund with only 50 stocks, like with QQQG. However, it's much harder to maintain quality when you hold 189 stocks, like with QGRO.

Overall, I think QGRO's quality is adequate but definitely up for debate. I can see readers wanting to combine a more established fund like SCHG with a less-diversified but higher-quality fund with low overlap like QQQG. As shown below in the ETF Overlap Matrix I updated on April 1, QQQG and SCHG overlap by only 21.21%. It's a more efficient complement than QGRO (32.31%) and far more efficient than just combining funds like IWF/SCHG/VUG/SPYG, as their overlap is around 75-80%.

QGRO: "Stable Growth" Value Screens Help

QGRO's "Stable Growth" sub-portfolio maximizes the value and profitability composite scores, subject to some constraints covering sector, quality, growth, and market cap. We see this show up in QGRO's valuation ratios, which are superior to its peers across the board. For example, it trades at 23.81x forward earnings and 19.05x trailing cash flow, which are about 5% and 17% less than the peer group average on a relative basis.

In my view, it's nice to keep valuation in mind, as it could provide some protection in market downturns. QGRO's new strategy wasn't tested in 2022, but I imagine it would have outperformed by a few percentage points as high P/E stocks were targeted most. Furthermore, with recession fears increasing, getting your portfolio's P/E ratio down a little bit makes sense.

Investment Recommendation

In summary, QGRO is a large-cap growth fund that sacrifices some quality in exchange for greater exposure to the diversification, growth, and value factors. Its overlap with popular funds like IWF, SCHG, VUG, and SPYG is low, so even if you aren't ready to give those up, adding QGRO is a quick way to offset some of the concentration risks you might have.

I believe other analysts have underestimated QGRO's strengths and have relied primarily on its mediocre historical performance to justify their conclusions. What they may not realize is that QGRO's strategy changed two years ago, and it has been a top-performing fund in its category ever since, without much help from mega-cap growth stocks. I think it's the equivalent of the JPMorgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF (JQUA) for the large-cap growth category, and I expect it will continue delivering solid results moving forward. Therefore, I'm reiterating my "buy" rating on QGRO, and I look forward to answering any questions you might have in the comments below.

  • The equity portfolio returned -0.25% 1 for the quarter versus -4.27% for the S&P 500 Index (SP500,SPX). The fixed income portfolio returned 2.91%1 versus 2.78% for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. The Fund allocation was 58.1% in equities, 41.1% in fixed income, and 0.8% cash.
  • At the sector level for the equity portfolio, the largest contributors to performance were financials and consumer staples, while consumer discretionary and communication services were the largest detractors.
  • As we have done in other periods when stock prices declined significantly, we are taking advantage of the opportunity to reposition the portfolio toward companies with the most attractive risk-adjusted returns.

TOP CONTRIBUTOR | DETRACTOR

Top contributor

American International Group (AIG) was the top contributor during the quarter. The company reported improved premium growth, strong underwriting results, and significant capital return during the period. We expect these trends to continue, which has the potential to lead to strong EPS (earnings per share) growth and improved returns. Over time, we expect the company’s return on equity to converge with peer levels. As returns continue to improve, we expect the valuation discount to the industry’s top peers to narrow.

Top detractor

Alphabet was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered company’s stock price declined despite having posted fourth-quarter 2024 earnings that were in line with consensus expectations. Search revenue growth remained strong, and management reiterated that the new “AI Overviews” feature is driving higher engagement with comparable monetization. The one miss during the quarter was in the Cloud segment, where revenue grew 30% year-over-year but fell slightly short of consensus expectations. We believe the shortfall was largely due to short-term capacity constraints and that the long-term growth outlook for Google Cloud remains robust. We continue to see Alphabet as a collection of great businesses that can further benefit from the company’s world class AI capabilities. With shares trading at just 15x our estimate of next year’s earnings per share, we believe the stock is meaningfully undervalued.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

New purchases

Molina Healthcare is a leading managed care company. Molina is the fourth largest player in managed Medicaid but has consistently delivered industry-leading growth and margins. In our view, this is thanks to the company’s exceptional management team and culture of operational excellence. We think Molina has a long runway for growth via continued share gains in Medicaid and untapped opportunities in their Medicare and Marketplace business segments. Recently, fundamentals have been pressured by an unprecedented redeterminations cycle in Medicaid, and valuations have compressed due to uncertainty around policy changes in a new political administration. We believe earnings pressure from redeterminations is temporary and that any Medicaid policy changes will prove manageable, providing us an opportunity to purchase shares at a meaningful discount to intrinsic value.

  • IONOS Group

Final sales

  • Auction Technology Group (OTCPK:ATHGF)
  • DS Smith (OTCPK:DITHF)
  • SoftwareONE Holding (OTCPK:SWONF)
  • Sulzer (OTCPK:SULZF)

FIRST QUARTER, 2025

  • The Fund’s Investor Share Class returned 8.24% versus 3.40% for the MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Index (net) for the quarter. Since its inception in November 1995, the Fund has returned 8.48%.
  • At the sector level, industrials and consumer staples were the largest contributors to performance, while real estate and consumer discretionary were the only detractors.
  • Geographically, the top three country allocations are 13.4% in the U.K., 11.3% in Germany and 10.6% in Italy. For the quarter, Germany, Australia and Denmark were the top contributors to relative performance. Italy, Belgium and Spain were top detractors from relative performance. Emerging markets accounted for 13.5% of the portfolio.
  • The Fund benefitted from its overweight exposure to European equities with its top seven contributors in the quarter all being European based companies.

TOP CONTRIBUTOR | DETRACTOR

Top contributor

ISS was the top contributor during the quarter. The Denmark-headquartered diversified support services company’s stock price rose after it posted solid fiscal-year 2024 earnings, which were broadly in line with consensus expectations. Better-than-expected topline growth was primarily a result of favorable pricing trends and elevated project work. In addition, management announced a 2.5 billion DKK (Danish krone) buyback program that is expected to be completed in 2025, which we view as significantly value positive and surpassed market expectations. We appreciate management’s focus on profitable growth and believe there is a significant runway for continued growth and modest margin expansion.

Top detractor

Travis Perkins was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.K.-based builder merchant’s stock price declined after it was announced that recently appointed CEO, Pete Redfern stepped down with immediate effect due to ill health. We were disappointed with the news as we believe Pete was implementing positive change as Travis attempts to improve its operational performance. While they search for a new CEO, the company will be managed by Geoff Drabble, (Chairman) who we hold in high regard (former CEO of Ashtead). Although Pete’s departure likely prolongs—and also increases—risks that could detriment a successful turnaround, we continue to be comfortable maintaining our position as Travis could see a material uplift in earnings from either a normalizing of the demand market in the U.K. or successful implementation of self-help measures.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

We exited four positions in the quarter: We sold Sulzer, DS Smith and Auction Technology as share price converged with our estimate of intrinsic value, and also exited our position in SoftwareOne given concerns over its proposed merger with Crayon.

New purchases

Ermenegildo Zegna is a global luxury fashion house and the world's largest luxury menswear specialist. Over the past decade, the company has enhanced the value of its portfolio by expanding into multiple brands while maintaining its world-class quality and creative designs. The company's core brand, Zegna, offers exposure to the highest category of luxury goods while brands Thom Browne and Tom Ford operate in a more fashion-forward luxury segment. Recently, slowing demand in China has weighed on results, particularly at Thom Browne. Despite these results, we appreciate management's actions to establish Zegna as an absolute luxury brand and address the issues at Thom Browne. In our view, there is healthy brand momentum for Zegna and results for Thom Browne have troughed. Additionally, we believe Tom Ford offers significant upside optionality that can be unlocked by a new creative director and management team. The current valuation does not reflect the long-term growth and normalized earnings profile of the company, providing the opportunity to purchase shares at a discounted valuation to publicly traded peers.

Gerresheimer AG is the world's largest manufacturer of pharmaceutical packaging and delivery systems. The company is in the midst of a transition from being a volume-oriented supplier to a valuedriven co-innovation partner with its pharmaceutical clients, a shift that we expect to drive faster growth, higher profitability and better returns over time. It is currently launching multiple new factories for its high value business that we anticipate will support results in the midterm. In addition, Gerresheimer is now reviewing its Moulded Glass business, with a potential disposal of this unit increasing the quality of the portfolio and narrowing the perception gap to its higher valued peers. Vial destocking post-COVID has caused near-term uncertainty in the market and provided the opportunity to purchase shares in the company at a discounted valuation to publicly traded peers and comparable private market transactions.

IONOS is a leading European internet service company that specializes in web presence and cloud computing solutions. IONOS commands leading market share positions in its core markets across Europe and offers a critical service to small businesses by providing a digital storefront, legitimacy and a platform to engage customers. In our view, this is an attractive industry with a sticky customer base that should benefit from structural growth tailwinds through continued digitization, AI adoption and pricing power. IONOS is further poised to begin harvesting investments made in cloud offerings that have temporarily depressed earnings and cash flow. Despite the company's attractive fundamental outlook, we were able to purchase shares in IONOS at a discounted valuation relative to publicly traded peers and private market valuations.

BNP Paribas was the top contributor during the quarter. The France-headquartered bank’s stock price rose throughout the period as it posted strong fiscal-year 2024 results. Performance was driven by strength in the Corporate and Institutional Banking (CIB) and Personal Finance segments. The company also demonstrated improved efficiency, with revenue growth outpacing cost growth. Given BNP’s strong underlying profitability and highly diversified business, we believe the bank is wellpositioned for sustained capital generation and long-term growth.

Top detractor

WPP Group was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.K.-headquartered advertising company’s stock price declined after it delivered weaker than expected fourth-quarter 2024 results and 2025 guidance. The results were impacted by client losses, disruptions from restructuring activity, and pullback in more discretionary client spending. Despite the headwinds, margins and free cash flow (FCF) remained resilient. While the economic environment remains uncertain, management believes WPP’s growth should start to recover in the back half of 2025, and their FCF generation should rebound as restructuring activities fade. While the past year has been a tough one for WPP, we continue to believe the business can deliver low single-digit growth and much improved FCF. WPP can also monetize equity assets like Kantar for material amounts. We continue to think the discounted valuation overly discounts the company’s fundamental value.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

Given attractive valuation levels across most international markets it was an active quarter for portfolio additions. We established five new positions including two Japan-based businesses.

New purchases

Asahi Group Holdings is a leading global beverage company and Japan's largest beer company. In our view, the Japanese beer market will see tailwinds over the coming years as a result of liquor tax reform and price increases following years of decline. With a strong CEO in Atsushi Katsuki and the country’s best-selling beer brand, we believe the company is uniquely positioned to capitalize on these tailwinds and expand domestic profits over the medium term. In addition, we like that Asahi commands attractive market positions in Australia and Central Europe, which support high margins and revenue growth. Lastly, after years of deleveraging following large acquisitions, we expect management to increase shareholder returns while continuing to realize the benefits of the company’s global procurement initiatives. Despite this compelling setup, we were able to purchase shares in this above-average business at a below-average valuation, in our view.

Flutter Entertainment is the world's largest online gambling company, boasting a portfolio of premier online sports betting and casino brands including FanDuel, Sportsbet, Pokerstars and Paddy Power. The company commands the number one market position across the vast majority of its revenue base driven by its best-in-class product. We appreciate that online gambling favors scaled players and believe that management has Flutter well-positioned to capitalize on strong secular growth tailwinds as the penetration of online gambling continues to increase. A recent sell-off in the share price provided the opportunity to buy shares in a company led by a management team that is renowned for top-notch execution and capital allocation.

Louis Vuitton Moët Hennessy (LVMH) is the world's largest luxury goods company with a portfolio of 75 brands, including prestigious houses Louis Vuitton and Christian Dior. Importantly, the company is focused on fashion and leather goods. This is one of the more attractive subcategories within the sector and where Vuitton has high brand desirability and scale, which drive industry leading profitability. The business is run by Bernard Arnault, who we consider to be one of the best CEOs in Europe, and who is fully aligned with shareholders given his family’s 41% ownership. The opportunity exists because of the recent fears around tariffs and the macro environment (initially in China and increasingly in the U.S.). We view this as a rare opportunity to purchase shares in LVMH at a discounted valuation to its peers and the company's own trading history. We expect that the industry will recover over the medium term, that recent innovations will help sustain attractive revenue growth and that self-help will serve to reaccelerate earnings growth.

Mitsubishi Estate is the largest owner of real estate in Tokyo's Marunouchi neighborhood. Marunouchi is the prime business district in Tokyo where office rental space has historically been characterized by low vacancy rates and rising rents, even in deflationary environments. In our view, Japan's shift to an inflationary environment will provide additional tailwinds to Mitsubishi's growing rental income in Marunouchi. We also like that the company has been strategically monetizing assets in other regions and returning funds to shareholders through buybacks and dividends, helping to boost the company's return on equity. We were happy to recently purchase shares at a discount to our estimate of net asset value and private market comparable transactions. We believe the gap between share price and intrinsic value should narrow as capital is returned to shareholders.

Rentokil Initial PLC is the largest pest control company globally. We think pest control is a good business that grows GDP plus with low volatility, earns attractive margins, and has low capital intensity with strong cash flow generation. As a market leader, Rentokil additionally benefits from scale advantages that drive lower procurement and customer acquisition costs, and greater customer monetization, thanks to the breadth of products and services the company can offer at a premium price. Due to industry fragmentation, Rentokil also has a strong pipeline of acquisitions with attractive economics. While most of Rentokil’s mergers and acquisitions are bolt-ons, they have occasionally done large deals. Their most recent large deal, the acquisition of Terminix, has gone poorly to-date and resulted in a share price decline in excess of the deal value. While Terminix’s integration will take several quarters to complete, we believe they will ultimately succeed and that the resulting business will be a stronger and more profitable one that can deliver growth in line with the industry's average. This should drive a re-rating in the shares towards peer and private market multiples. We believe the market has overly discounted the business’s future prospects.

data.title: The Launch, Lurch, Lift-Off Cycle
data.description:
Stocks rose today, closing out one of the most head-spinning weeks in recent memory - coming off an equally nutty one last week. By the closing bell, the S&P 500 had booked its biggest weekly gain since October 2023 and the Nasdaq since November 2022.

Level Change 4/11/25 (%)

+1.6 Dow

+2.1 Nasdaq

+1.9 Nasdaq 100

+1.8 S&P 500

+1.4 S&P 400

+1.3 S&P 600

The latest trade war salvo came from China, which threw sharp elbows against America’s 145% tariff on Chinese goods with a 125% broadside of its own. At least it mentioned it will ignore future US increases, which seem to appear more often than bedbugs in a budget motel.

Chinese leader Xi Jinping said his country is “not afraid,” and China’s Commerce Ministry waved off Washington’s ever-escalating tariff theatrics as “nothing more than a numbers game, with no real economic significance.” It ridiculed Team Trump’s weaponization of tariffs “as a tool of bullying and coercion, turning itself into a joke.”

Although there does seem to be some economic significance going around.

US Treasuries are going pale in the face as the world begins to question whether America’s economic steering wheel is attached to anything at all. Investors dumped US government bonds this week, driving prices lower and - by the laws of inverse correlation between price and yield - yields higher. The 30-year yield posted its biggest weekly jump since 1982.

Instead of US government bonds, the new safe haven appears to be German and Japanese bonds. This switcheroo coincides with the dollar’s steady trip fall the stairs (down more than 3% this week), suggesting the era of US exceptionalism may soon get its closing credits.

Things are getting so nervy that the Fed needed to assure everyone it will step in if markets seize or liquidity dries up.

Boston Fed President Susan Collins repeated the do-nothing official stance on rates, but added music to the message. She said the Fed has tools for market dysfunction and low liquidity, and is “absolutely prepared to move.” Investors liked the sound of that, and handed her partial credit for today’s bounce.

New York Fed President John Williams expects US economic growth to slip under 1% this year, as the trade war drives up inflation and unemployment. Still, it’s go-slow for him and all the Fedsters on the tightrope strung over a fog machine.

Meanwhile, economic data keep arriving like bills after a vacation: unwelcome and piling up.

April preliminary Michigan consumer sentiment missed expectations and notched its fourth straight monthly decline. Inflation expectations rose, with the one-year up 1.7 percentage points to 6.7%, a big jump from 5% in March to its highest since 1981.

Longer-term (5 to 10 years) edged up 0.3 percentage points to 4.4%, quietly undermining the Fed’s narrative that long-term inflation expectations remain “well-anchored.” The anchor, it seems, is sliding through sand, and the boat’s drifting steadily from the Fed’s 2% harbor.

Maybe the real hard data will come to the rescue. Q1 earnings season started today, and so far, so good. JPMorgan (JPM +4%) grew profit 9%, while Wells Fargo (WFC -1%) grew it 6%. Morgan Stanley (MS +1.4%) beat earnings expectations. BlackRock (BLK +2.3%) hit record assets of $11.6T. Apparently, someone out there still has money.

JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon noted that clients are becoming “more cautious” as geopolitical and trade tensions rise. They seem to dislike inflation, too. He warned that “the economy is facing considerable turbulence,” and his bank is braced “for a wide range of scenarios.” Some of them, one hopes, don’t involve helmets and barf bags.

Those are the winds of Wall Street tonight, where tariffs bite and bond traders blink harder than someone biting into wasabi they thought was guac.

data.title: Undervalued Yen Is Also One Of The Few 'Safe Havens'
data.description:
In today's unsettling macroeconomic environment, where even US Treasury Bonds no longer seem like a safe haven, few assets remain uncorrelated to the broader market. While predicting currency movements can be challenging, the Japanese Yen stands out as a potential beneficiary in this market turmoil and geopolitical context. Additionally, the wide interest rate differential between U.S. and Japanese interest rates, which has been a key factor holding back the Yen, may be nearing its end, potentially paving the way for a reversal in the currency’s trajectory. I recommend taking a long position on the JPY/USD currency pair as part of a long-term or medium-term strategy.

Investors shouldn't expect high double-digit returns, as the objective of this trade is mainly protective. For those without access to foreign exchange or interest rate derivatives, ETFs like NYSE:"FXY" (with a modest expense ratio of 0.4%) provide a practical vehicle to gain exposure.

Background and currency valuation methods

After decades of the Yen strengthening against the USD, which started because of the Japanese economic miracle (1960s), since 2010, the yen has weakened against the USD. Ultra-loose monetary policy by the Bank of Japan (BOJ) to foster its export-driven economy contributed to the Yen weakness. This has been exacerbated by the Fed's tightening stance since 2021, which intensified carry-trade dynamics (investors borrowing in yen and investing in higher-yielding USD bonds). Despite the BOJ's interventions, the yen continued to depreciate, unlike the Swiss franc, which appreciated. As a result, Japanese goods and services have become extremely cheap in USD terms, with Tokyo now more affordable than many major Latin American cities.

USD/JPY (Japanese yens per USD) 1971 – 2025

In this article, I will be using the two main theories used to value currencies: Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) and Interest Rate Parity (IRP).

PPP is an economic theory that suggests that in the long run, exchange rates should adjust so that a basket of goods costs the same in different countries when measured in a common currency. As baskets of goods differ by countries, The Economist uses the McDonald's Big-Mac burger to calculate currency valuations in different parts of the world.

The second most used approach is IRP. This theory states that currency forward (exchange rate in the future) will depend on the spread between interest rates (as per the formula below). If bonds from Country A offer a 2% higher yield than those from Country B, IRP predicts that Country A’s currency should depreciate by approximately 2%. Otherwise, arbitrage opportunities would emerge.

PPP and IRP: reality check

Is the Yen cheap according to PPP? Absolutely! According to the Economist Big-Mc Index, Japan has the most undervalued currency of the G7 and, together with India, the most of the G20. According to this index, adjusted by GDP per capita, “A Big Mac costs 46.3% less in Tokyo ($3.11) than in the United States ($5.79) at market exchange rates (January). Based on differences in GDP per person, a Big Mac should cost 7.4% less. This suggests the Japanese yen is 42% undervalued.”, according to The Economist Metric.

Purchasing Power Parity (USD to Japanese Yen)

Despite this historically significant undervaluation (according to PPP theory), the interest rate differential between the US and Japan has prevented the Yen from strengthening as capital inflows have continued to enter the US. American successful tech giants, AI revolution has also helped this process. And in the last four years, the US restrictive monetary policy with many FED rate hikes, has fostered even further the Yen weakness. The latter has occurred despite the BOJ having loosened the yield curve control. Also, between 2022-2024, the BoJ spent over USD160 billion in FX interventions to slow the yen’s depreciation, with limited success.

What about IRP? Following Fed tightening in 2021, US short-term (3-year bonds) rates increased, generating a spread of 150bps vs. Japanese bonds. As a result, the yen depreciated from around 100 Yen per USD to almost 160 yen. If this spread narrows, as it happened during the 2009 financial crisis, the Yen is likely to strengthen vs. the dollar.

JPY vs. US/Japan 3-Year Yield Spread favors inflows to US markets

Are we entering a new era where the Yen will revalue against the USD?

The American administration has requested that countries with significant trade surpluses reduce it or face “reciprocal tariffs”. If tariffs cause calamity in financial markets as we have witnessed, a rebalancing of trade deficits is likely to come as well through exchange rates, as it happened in the 1980s. Finally, this will be a much healthier and “clean” solution, avoiding free-market distortions and free-trade treaty violations.

Risks

I am aware that forecasting currency is one of the most difficult tasks in economic history. Particularly in the short term, anything can happen! And sometimes, currencies can remain overvalued or undervalued for extremely long periods of time due to interest rate differentials, capital controls, and many other forces. Betting the Yen against the dollar has been incredibly unsuccessful.

If history serves as a lesson, following the Plaza Hotel Accord in 1985, although both Germany and Japan devalued their currencies, only in the case of Germany was it useful as a tool to reduce the American trade deficit. In the case of Japan, cultural and other factors prevented more buying of American products in Japan.

Also, long-term rates in Japan are low as there is a high demand for fixed-income securities: an aging population requires these assets to fund pensions. Also, an aging population tends to save more and consume less. This creates a “saving glut”, which is a structural factor behind low interest rates in Japan.

Conclusions and final thoughts

I believe that the current trade war started by the American administration should end in some kind of political agreement like the “Plaza Hotel Accord”, where other G7 countries let their currency devalue against the USD to correct imbalances in the 1980s. It is already happening now with the Euro. This time, it could happen with many Asian currencies, which are among the most undervalued currencies in the world.

From an IRP perspective, the US self-inflicted potential recession and price drops in commodities (e.g., oil, copper) will likely lead to a bearish Fed, willing to reduce interest rates more quickly, which could result in USD weakness against the Yen. This could bring USD/JPY towards levels near 100, helping to reduce the American trade and current account deficit with Japan.

If you want to play long YEN/USD and don't have access to currency or interest rate derivatives markets, you can do it through FXY. This ETF has call options, though not very liquid, which are an alternative.

data.title: Country ETFs Since Liberation Day And Pause Day
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPY) rallied 5.7% this week but remains down 5.4% since the close on "Liberation Day" on 4/2. Below is a look at the performance of 45 country ETFs traded on US exchanges since 4/2 and since the close on 4/8 before President Trump announced the 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for all countries except China.

The average country ETF is down 4% since 4/2, so SPY has underperformed that since Trump's Rose Garden announcement. Since the close on 4/8 before the pause, the average country ETF has bounced back 7.9%, so SPY has underperformed slightly on the bounce-back as well.

The country ETFs that have bounced the most since the pause are ones you might expect. Asian countries like Vietnam (VNAM) and Thailand (THD) had some of the harshest reciprocal tariffs announced on Liberation Day, and since the pause, these two have bounced back 16.7% and 14.1%, respectively. Thailand (THD) is now down just 1.2% since Liberation Day.

The third-best country ETF since 4/8 has been Argentina (ARGT) with a gain of 12.8%. ARGT gained 6.5% on Friday to end this week.

China - the country most punished by Trump's tariffs - has bounced back for US investors that own the MSCI China ETF (MCHI) since the pause, even though Trump hiked China's tariff rate to 125% on Wednesday. MCHI is up 9.9% since the close on 4/8 versus SPY's gain of 7.5%. Since Liberation Day on 4/2, MCHI is down 8.3%, though, compared to SPY's decline of 5.4%.

There are four country ETFs down less than 1% since Liberation Day: South Korea (EWY), Switzerland (EWL), New Zealand (ENZL), and Belgium (EWK). Colombia (GXG) and Hong Kong (EWH) head into the weekend as the only country ETFs still down 10%+ since 4/2.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Falls Further As Inflation Expectations Soar
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer confidence took another hit this month, primarily due to escalating worries about trade wars, inflation, and the labor market. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index plummeted to 50.8 in April, its second-lowest reading on record, surpassed only by June 2022.

This represents a significant 10.9% (6.2 point) decline from March, undershooting the anticipated 54.0. Furthermore, consumer sentiment has decreased by 34.2% (26.4 points) compared to a year ago, marking the largest annual decline since July 2022.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regards to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month; a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment fell for the fourth straight month, plunging 11% from March. This decline was, like the last month’s, pervasive and unanimous across age, income, education, geographic region, and political affiliation. Sentiment has now lost more than 30% since December 2024 amid growing worries about trade war developments that have oscillated over the course of the year. Consumers report multiple warning signs that raise the risk of recession: expectations for business conditions, personal finances, incomes, inflation, and labor markets all continued to deteriorate this month. The share of consumers expecting unemployment to rise in the year ahead increased for the fifth consecutive month and is now more than double the November 2024 reading and the highest since 2009. This lack of labor market confidence lies in sharp contrast to the past several years, when robust spending was supported primarily by strong labor markets and incomes. Note that interviews for this release were conducted between March 25 and April 8, closing prior to the April 9 tariff partial reversal.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 50.8, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 39.9% below its average reading of 84.5 (arithmetic mean) and 39.1% below its geometric mean of 83.4, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 0th percentile of the 568 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 6.2-point decrease from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In April, the CECI dropped to 56.5, the lowest reading since June 2022. This represents an 11.4% decline from the previous month and a 28.5% decline from a year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 61.5.

Meanwhile, the CEI fell to 47.2, the lowest reading since 1980. This represents a 10.3% decline from the prior month and a 37.9% drop from one year ago. This reading was worse than the forecast of 50.8.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations surged from 5.0% last month to 6.7% this month, the highest reading since 1981 and marking four consecutive months of unusually large increases of 0.5 percentage points or more. This month’s rise was seen across all three political affiliations. Long-run inflation expectations climbed from 4.1% in March to 4.4% in April, reflecting a particularly large jump among independents.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).
